title: Positive Integer Solutions of a Linear Equation
text:
Find the number of positive integer solutions of
\[
x+2y+3z=15,
\]
where
\[
x,y,z\in\mathbb{Z}^{+}.
\]

title: Positive Integer Solutions of a Linear Equation
text:
Find the number of positive integer solutions of
\[
x+2y+3z=15,
\]
where
\[
x,y,z\in\mathbb{Z}^{+}.
\]

title: Positive Integer Solutions of a Linear Equation
text:
Counting the Non-negative Integer Solutions
For every fixed value of \(c\),
\[
a+2b=9-3c.
\]
Since \(a,b,c\geq 0\), the possible values of \(c\) are
\[
c=0,1,2,3.
\]
For an equation of the form
\[
a+2b=N,
\]
we have
\[
a=N-2b.
\]
Therefore, \(b\) may take the values
\[
0,1,2,\ldots,\left\lfloor\frac{N}{2}\right\rfloor.
\]
Hence, the number of non-negative integer solutions is
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{N}{2}\right\rfloor+1.
\]
Now consider each value of \(c\).

When \(c=0\)
\[
a+2b=9.
\]
Number of solutions:
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{9}{2}\right\rfloor+1=5.
\]


When \(c=1\)
\[
a+2b=6.
\]
Number of solutions:
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{6}{2}\right\rfloor+1=4.
\]


When \(c=2\)
\[
a+2b=3.
\]
Number of solutions:
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{3}{2}\right\rfloor+1=2.
\]


When \(c=3\)
\[
a+2b=0.
\]
Number of solutions:
\[
1.
\]
Therefore,
\[
5+4+2+1=12.
\]
Hence,
\[
\boxed{12}
\]

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Counting integer solutions is a central idea in algebra, number theory, and combinatorics. Here the word positive matters: each variable must be at least \(1\), not merely non-negative.
We will count the solutions in four complementary ways. The mathematics supplies the proof; Python provides an independent verification.

Difficulty: Intermediate Prerequisites: Basic algebra, inequalities, and elementary counting Estimated reading time: 8 minutes

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Counting integer solutions is a central idea in algebra, number theory, and combinatorics. Here the word positive matters: each variable must be at least \(1\), not merely non-negative.
We will count the solutions in four complementary ways. The mathematics supplies the proof; Python provides an independent verification.

Difficulty: Intermediate Prerequisites: Basic algebra, inequalities, and elementary counting Estimated reading time: 8 minutes

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Problem
Find the number of positive integer solutions of
\[
x+2y+3z=15,
\]
where \(x,y,z\in\mathbb Z^+\). The answer will be
\[
\boxed{12}.
\]

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
section: Method 1: Fix One Variable and Count
text:
Method 1: Fix One Variable and Count
Solving for \(x\) gives
\[
x=15-2y-3z.
\]
Since \(x\geq1\), we have \(2y+3z\leq14\). Because \(y\geq1\), this gives \(2+3z\leq14\), so \(z\leq4\). Thus \(z=1,2,3,4\).
For each fixed \(z\), count the positive values of \(y\) for which \(x=15-2y-3z\) stays positive.









Value of \(z\)
Equation for \(x\) and \(y\)
Allowed values of \(y\)
Number of solutions




\(1\)
\(x+2y=12\)
\(1,2,3,4,5\)
\(5\)


\(2\)
\(x+2y=9\)
\(1,2,3,4\)
\(4\)


\(3\)
\(x+2y=6\)
\(1,2\)
\(2\)


\(4\)
\(x+2y=3\)
\(1\)
\(1\)



Therefore,
\[
5+4+2+1=\boxed{12}.
\]

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
section: Method 2: Convert Positive Integers to Non-negative Integers
text:
Method 2: Convert Positive Integers to Non-negative Integers
Write
\[
x=a+1,\qquad y=b+1,\qquad z=c+1,
\]
where \(a,b,c\geq0\). Substitution gives
\[
(a+1)+2(b+1)+3(c+1)=15,
\]
and hence
\[
a+2b+3c=9.
\]
For fixed \(c\), this becomes \(a+2b=9-3c\). An equation \(a+2b=N\), with \(a,b\geq0\), has \(\left\lfloor N/2\right\rfloor+1\) solutions because \(b\) ranges from \(0\) to \(\left\lfloor N/2\right\rfloor\).



\(c\)
Equation
Number of solutions




\(0\)
\(a+2b=9\)
\(\left\lfloor9/2\right\rfloor+1=5\)


\(1\)
\(a+2b=6\)
\(\left\lfloor6/2\right\rfloor+1=4\)


\(2\)
\(a+2b=3\)
\(\left\lfloor3/2\right\rfloor+1=2\)


\(3\)
\(a+2b=0\)
\(1\)



Again,
\[
5+4+2+1=\boxed{12}.
\]

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Method 3: Direct Floor-Function Formula
For fixed positive integer \(z\), positivity of \(x\) implies
\[
15-2y-3z\geq1,
\]
so
\[
y\leq\frac{14-3z}{2}.
\]
The number of positive integer values of \(y\) is therefore
\[
\left\lfloor\frac{14-3z}{2}\right\rfloor.
\]
Since \(z=1,2,3,4\),
\[
N=\sum_{z=1}^{4}\left\lfloor\frac{14-3z}{2}\right\rfloor.
\]
Evaluating the sum,
\[
N=
\left\lfloor\frac{11}{2}\right\rfloor+
\left\lfloor\frac{8}{2}\right\rfloor+
\left\lfloor\frac{5}{2}\right\rfloor+
\left\lfloor\frac{2}{2}\right\rfloor
=5+4+2+1=\boxed{12}.
\]

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Method 4: Generating Functions
The positive values of \(x\), \(2y\), and \(3z\) are encoded respectively by
\[
\frac{t}{1-t},\qquad
\frac{t^2}{1-t^2},\qquad
\frac{t^3}{1-t^3}.
\]
Their product is
\[
G(t)=
\left(\frac{t}{1-t}\right)
\left(\frac{t^2}{1-t^2}\right)
\left(\frac{t^3}{1-t^3}\right)
=\frac{t^6}{(1-t)(1-t^2)(1-t^3)}.
\]
Choosing \(t^x\), \(t^{2y}\), and \(t^{3z}\) from the three factors produces
\[
t^x\cdot t^{2y}\cdot t^{3z}=t^{x+2y+3z}.
\]
Thus every positive solution contributes exactly one \(t^{15}\) term, so the answer is
\[
[t^{15}]G(t)
=[t^9]\frac{1}{(1-t)(1-t^2)(1-t^3)}.
\]
That coefficient is \(12\), in agreement with the direct counts.

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Python Verification
The following program uses the equation to compute \(x\) after choosing positive \(y\) and \(z\). It prints the triples in the same order as the hand-counting method.


Hide code
solutions = []

for z in range(1, 5):
    for y in range(1, 16):
        x = 15 - 2 * y - 3 * z
        if x > 0:
            solution = (x, y, z)
            solutions.append(solution)
            print(solution)

print("Total number of solutions:", len(solutions))


(10, 1, 1)
(8, 2, 1)
(6, 3, 1)
(4, 4, 1)
(2, 5, 1)
(7, 1, 2)
(5, 2, 2)
(3, 3, 2)
(1, 4, 2)
(4, 1, 3)
(2, 2, 3)
(1, 1, 4)
Total number of solutions: 12


The program confirms the count, but the earlier methods explain why the count is \(12\).

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Complete List of Solutions
The complete solution list is
\[
\begin{aligned}
z=1:&\quad (10,1,1),\ (8,2,1),\ (6,3,1),\ (4,4,1),\ (2,5,1),\\
z=2:&\quad (7,1,2),\ (5,2,2),\ (3,3,2),\ (1,4,2),\\
z=3:&\quad (4,1,3),\ (2,2,3),\\
z=4:&\quad (1,1,4).
\end{aligned}
\]
Every listed triple satisfies \(x+2y+3z=15\), and the list contains \(5+4+2+1=12\) triples.

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Comparison of Methods








Method
Main idea
Best use




Fix one variable
Count directly after fixing \(z\)
Small equations and hand calculations


Shift variables
Replace positivity by non-negativity
Connecting with standard combinatorics


Floor formula
Express each row count compactly
General formulas


Generating functions
Read a coefficient from a product of series
Weighted counting problems

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Conclusion
The four methods illuminate the same result from different angles: direct counting is concrete, shifting variables connects the problem to non-negative solutions, floor functions compress the count, and generating functions give a powerful general framework. Python verifies the final list.
\[
\boxed{12\text{ positive integer solutions}}.
\]

title: Four Ways to Count Positive Integer Solutions
text:
Try It Yourself

Find the number of positive integer solutions of \(x+2y+3z=18\).
Find the number of non-negative integer solutions of \(a+2b+3c=10\).
Find the number of positive integer solutions of \(x+3y+4z=20\).







TipHint for Problem 1





Fix \(z\) first. For each allowed value of \(z\), count the positive values of \(y\) that keep \(x=18-2y-3z\) positive.









NoteSolution for Problem 1





The possible values are \(z=1,2,3,4,5\). The row counts are \(7,5,4,2,1\), so the total is
\[
7+5+4+2+1=\boxed{19}.
\]









TipHint for Problem 2





Fix \(c=0,1,2,3\). For each \(c\), solve \(a+2b=10-3c\) with \(a,b\geq0\).









NoteSolution for Problem 2





The row counts are \(6,4,3,1\), giving
\[
6+4+3+1=\boxed{14}.
\]









TipHint for Problem 3





Use \(x=20-3y-4z\) and count the positive values of \(y\) for each possible positive \(z\).









NoteSolution for Problem 3





For \(z=1,2,3,4\), the possible values of \(y\) number \(5,3,2,1\) respectively. Hence the total is
\[
5+3+2+1=\boxed{11}.
\]

PyMathon brings Mathematics, Python, and Problem Solving together in one place.
Here you will find concise explanations that move from a clear idea to a complete solution, with computation used to verify rather than replace reasoning.
About Ashish K. Srivastava

title: About
text:
I am a mathematics enthusiast and curious problem solver who enjoys exploring ideas with clarity, structure, and creativity.
Through PyMathon, I combine classical reasoning with modern computational tools such as Python, SymPy, NumPy, Matplotlib, and Manim. My aim is to explain ideas clearly, rigorously, and visually while preserving the discipline of traditional step-by-step problem solving.

title: About
About
text:
My Teaching Philosophy
I believe that understanding must come before memorisation. A formula becomes truly useful only when a student understands where it comes from and when it applies.
Traditional step-by-step reasoning remains central to my teaching. I encourage students to write complete arguments, examine more than one solution method, and develop a visual understanding wherever possible. Algebraic, geometric, combinatorial, and computational viewpoints can often illuminate different parts of the same problem.
I use computation responsibly: Python can test examples, reveal patterns, verify calculations, and create illuminating visualisations, but it should support mathematical proof rather than replace it.

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text: The Collatz rule is easy to state, but its long-term behaviour is still not understood. We begin with the rule itself, then use it to solve the finite search problem posed in Project Euler Problem 14.

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text:
The Collatz Process
For a positive integer, apply one of two operations: halve an even number, and replace an odd number by three times the number plus one. Repeating this instruction produces a Collatz sequence, also called an orbit.
Let \(\mathbb{N}=\{1,2,3,\ldots\}\). The Collatz map is
\[
T(n)=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{n}{2}, & \text{if } n \text{ is even},\\[6pt]
3n+1, & \text{if } n \text{ is odd}.
\end{cases}
\]
Starting at \(n\), the successive values are \(n,T(n),T^2(n),\ldots\). Here \(T^k(n)\) means that the map has been applied \(k\) times.

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text:
The Collatz Conjecture
The conjecture states that every positive starting value eventually reaches \(1\):
\[
\forall n\in\mathbb{N},\quad
\exists k\geq 0
\text{ such that }
T^k(n)=1.
\]
Once the value \(1\) is reached, iteration continues around the familiar cycle
\[
1\rightarrow4\rightarrow2\rightarrow1.
\]
For chain lengths, we stop at the first occurrence of \(1\).

Why this is still a conjecture
Computers have checked the rule for extraordinarily many starting values. That is convincing evidence, but it is not a proof. A finite computation can only check finitely many integers; the statement concerns every positive integer.
A proof would have to rule out both an orbit that grows without bound and a non-trivial cycle other than \(1\to4\to2\to1\). Neither has been ruled out for all positive integers. The original Collatz conjecture therefore remains a conjecture, not a theorem.


A first example: \(n=13\)
Applying the rule carefully gives
\[
13\rightarrow40\rightarrow20\rightarrow10\rightarrow5
\rightarrow16\rightarrow8\rightarrow4\rightarrow2\rightarrow1.
\]
This sequence contains
\[
10\text{ terms}
\]
and takes
\[
9\text{ steps}.
\]
The distinction matters throughout this article:
\[
\text{number of steps}
=
\text{number of terms}-1.
\]

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text:
Generating Collatz Sequences with Python
Before writing a search program, we should be able to generate one orbit correctly. The function below checks that its input is a positive integer and returns the sequence up to the first \(1\).


Hide code
def collatz_sequence(n):
    """Return the Collatz sequence from positive integer n to its first 1."""
    if not isinstance(n, int) or isinstance(n, bool) or n < 1:
        raise ValueError("n must be a positive integer")

    sequence = [n]
    while n != 1:
        if n % 2 == 0:
            n //= 2
        else:
            n = 3 * n + 1
        sequence.append(n)
    return sequence


sequence_13 = collatz_sequence(13)
print(sequence_13)
print(f"Terms: {len(sequence_13)}")
print(f"Steps: {len(sequence_13) - 1}")


[13, 40, 20, 10, 5, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1]
Terms: 10
Steps: 9


The output agrees with the hand calculation: \(13\) has ten terms and nine steps.
For a few selected values, it is useful to record the chain length and the largest value reached. This is experimental verification of particular cases, not a proof of the conjecture.


Hide code
def collatz_summary(n):
    """Return basic information about the Collatz sequence starting at n."""
    sequence = collatz_sequence(n)
    return {
        "start": n,
        "terms": len(sequence),
        "steps": len(sequence) - 1,
        "maximum": max(sequence),
    }


test_values = [6, 10, 13, 19, 27, 97]
summaries = [collatz_summary(n) for n in test_values]
for summary in summaries:
    print(summary)


{'start': 6, 'terms': 9, 'steps': 8, 'maximum': 16}
{'start': 10, 'terms': 7, 'steps': 6, 'maximum': 16}
{'start': 13, 'terms': 10, 'steps': 9, 'maximum': 40}
{'start': 19, 'terms': 21, 'steps': 20, 'maximum': 88}
{'start': 27, 'terms': 112, 'steps': 111, 'maximum': 9232}
{'start': 97, 'terms': 119, 'steps': 118, 'maximum': 9232}

The chain beginning at \(27\) is worth noticing. It starts small, rises as high as \(9232\), and only then returns to \(1\). This is a useful reminder that the rule does not produce a steadily decreasing sequence.


Hide code
sequence_27 = collatz_sequence(27)
print(f"Terms: {len(sequence_27)}")
print(f"Steps: {len(sequence_27) - 1}")
print(f"Maximum value: {max(sequence_27)}")


Terms: 112
Steps: 111
Maximum value: 9232



Trajectory for \(n=27\)
A plot makes the rise and fall of this orbit easier to see.


Hide code
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'svg'
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("#0c1521")
ax.set_facecolor("#101d2d")
ax.plot(range(len(sequence_27)), sequence_27, linewidth=1.5, color="#a9ecf4")
ax.set_xlabel("Step number", color="#e7edf4")
ax.set_ylabel("Term value", color="#e7edf4")
ax.set_title("Collatz trajectory for n = 27", color="#f1f6fb")
ax.tick_params(colors="#d5e1eb")
for spine in ax.spines.values():
    spine.set_color("#5d7590")
ax.grid(True, color="#7890a8", alpha=0.25)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text:
Project Euler Problem 14
The Project Euler question is finite. It asks:

Among all starting integers satisfying
\[
1\leq n<1{,}000{,}000,
\]
find the starting value that produces the longest Collatz chain before reaching \(1\).

Only the starting number must be below one million. Terms later in the chain may be much larger. This is important: the eventual winner rises to more than two billion.

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
section: The Mathematical Structure of the Search
text:
The Mathematical Structure of the Search
Define
\[
L(n)=\text{number of terms from }n\text{ to the first occurrence of }1.
\]
Then
\[
L(1)=1
\]
and, for \(n>1\),
\[
L(n)=1+L(T(n)).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
L(n)=
\begin{cases}
1+L\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right),
& n\text{ even},\\[8pt]
1+L(3n+1),
& n\text{ odd}.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus the required value is
\[
n_{\max}
=
\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{1\leq n<1{,}000{,}000}L(n).
\]
The key observation is that chains share tails. Once a chain reaches a previously studied value, the remaining work is already known. For example, if \(L(10)\) is known, then
\[
L(20)=1+L(10),
\]
\[
L(40)=1+L(20),
\]
and
\[
L(13)=1+L(40).
\]
This recurrence is the mathematical reason that memoization works. Before we write the efficient program, it is useful to see the direct method.

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text:
A Direct Python Solution
The direct method constructs a complete chain for every possible starting value. It is correct, but it repeatedly computes common tails such as \(40\to20\to10\to\cdots\to1\).


Hide code
def longest_collatz_naive(limit):
    """Return the start and term count of the longest chain below limit."""
    if not isinstance(limit, int) or isinstance(limit, bool) or limit <= 1:
        raise ValueError("limit must be an integer greater than 1")

    best_start, best_length = 1, 1
    for n in range(1, limit):
        current_length = len(collatz_sequence(n))
        if current_length > best_length:
            best_start, best_length = n, current_length
    return best_start, best_length


print(longest_collatz_naive(1_000))


(871, 179)


For a small limit this is perfectly adequate. At one million starts, however, the repeated tails become expensive. The recurrence for \(L(n)\) tells us exactly what to store.

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text:
Memoization
Start with the known value \(L(1)=1\). When a new path reaches a value already stored in a cache, work backwards through the new part of the path. Each predecessor has a chain one term longer than its successor.


Hide code
def collatz_length(n, cache):
    """Return the term count for n, extending cache along a new tail."""
    if not isinstance(n, int) or isinstance(n, bool) or n < 1:
        raise ValueError("n must be a positive integer")

    original_n = n
    path = []
    while n not in cache:
        path.append(n)
        if n % 2 == 0:
            n //= 2
        else:
            n = 3 * n + 1

    known_length = cache[n]
    for value in reversed(path):
        known_length += 1
        cache[value] = known_length
    return cache[original_n]


def longest_collatz(limit):
    """Return the start, term count, and cache for chains below limit."""
    if not isinstance(limit, int) or isinstance(limit, bool) or limit <= 1:
        raise ValueError("limit must be an integer greater than 1")

    cache = {1: 1}
    best_start, best_length = 1, 1
    for n in range(1, limit):
        current_length = collatz_length(n, cache)
        if current_length > best_length:
            best_start, best_length = n, current_length
    return best_start, best_length, cache


Now we can translate the recurrence into Python. The following finite computation solves the Project Euler search; it does not claim to prove the Collatz conjecture.


Hide code
best_start, best_length, length_cache = longest_collatz(1_000_000)
print(f"Starting number: {best_start}")
print(f"Number of terms: {best_length}")
print(f"Number of steps: {best_length - 1}")


Starting number: 837799
Number of terms: 525
Number of steps: 524








NoteFinal result



\[
\boxed{837799}
\]
is the starting value below one million with the longest Collatz chain. The chain contains
\[
\boxed{525\text{ terms}}
\]
and requires
\[
\boxed{524\text{ steps}}.
\]

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text:
Inspecting the Winning Sequence
The winning chain begins with large oscillations but ends in the familiar tail through \(40,20,10,5,\ldots,1\).


Hide code
winning_sequence = collatz_sequence(best_start)
print("First 15 terms:")
print(winning_sequence[:15])
print("\nLast 15 terms:")
print(winning_sequence[-15:])
print(f"\nMaximum value reached: {max(winning_sequence):,}")


First 15 terms:
[837799, 2513398, 1256699, 3770098, 1885049, 5655148, 2827574, 1413787, 4241362, 2120681, 6362044, 3181022, 1590511, 4771534, 2385767]

Last 15 terms:
[70, 35, 106, 53, 160, 80, 40, 20, 10, 5, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1]

Maximum value reached: 2,974,984,576




Hide code
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'svg'
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("#0c1521")
ax.set_facecolor("#101d2d")
ax.plot(range(len(winning_sequence)), winning_sequence, linewidth=1.35, color="#ffe09a")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Step number", color="#e7edf4")
ax.set_ylabel("Term value (logarithmic scale)", color="#e7edf4")
ax.set_title("Collatz trajectory for n = 837,799", color="#f1f6fb")
ax.tick_params(colors="#d5e1eb")
for spine in ax.spines.values():
    spine.set_color("#5d7590")
ax.grid(True, color="#7890a8", alpha=0.25)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The logarithmic vertical scale is necessary here: the chain reaches \(2{,}974{,}984{,}576\), far above its starting value.

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
section: Mathematical Ideas Connected with the Collatz Conjecture
text:
Mathematical Ideas Connected with the Collatz Conjecture
The Collatz map is a discrete dynamical system: it studies the long-term behaviour of repeatedly applying a function on the positive integers. Questions about iteration include whether an orbit reaches a cycle, whether it escapes, and how long it takes to fall below its starting value.
The total stopping time is
\[
\sigma_\infty(n)=\min\{k\geq0:T^k(n)=1\}.
\]
When the orbit reaches \(1\), \(L(n)=\sigma_\infty(n)+1\). A related stopping time is the first \(k\geq1\) for which \(T^k(n)<n\). Proving that every starting value eventually falls below itself would give a route to the conjecture by strong induction, but that statement is also unproved.
For odd \(n\), the accelerated Collatz map, often called the Syracuse map, divides all powers of two out of \(3n+1\) at once:
\[
S(n)=\frac{3n+1}{2^{\nu_2(3n+1)}}.
\]
Here \(\nu_2(m)\) is the exponent of \(2\) in \(m\). The map sends odd integers directly to odd integers. Parity sequences, or parity vectors, record the even and odd decisions along an orbit. Once a finite parity pattern is fixed, the iterate has an affine form involving powers of \(3\) and \(2\); the difficulty is that the pattern depends on the orbit itself.
The directed Collatz graph has an arrow \(n\longrightarrow T(n)\) from every positive integer. Reversing the arrows produces inverse Collatz trees, which make shared tails visible. Probabilistic heuristics model the powers of two dividing \(3n+1\) as if they were random; they help explain the observed downward tendency, but they are not proofs because actual parity events are deterministic and dependent.
There are important partial results. Terence Tao proved that, in a precise logarithmic-density sense, almost all orbits attain values below any function that tends to infinity. “Almost all” is not “all”, so this does not settle the original conjecture. Generalized Collatz problems can even be undecidable in some settings. That result concerns generalized systems; it does not show that the original Collatz conjecture is undecidable.

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text:
Conclusion
The Collatz conjecture remains open. Project Euler Problem 14 is nevertheless a finite problem, and the recurrence
\[
L(n)=1+L(T(n))
\]
turns its repeated tails into a practical memoized computation. The longest chain below one million starts at \(837799\) and has \(525\) terms.

title: Longest Collatz Sequence
text:
References

Project Euler, Problem 14: Longest Collatz Sequence
Jeffrey C. Lagarias, The \(3x+1\) Problem: An Overview
Terence Tao, Almost All Orbits of the Collatz Map Attain Almost Bounded Values
Terence Tao, expository discussion of the result
Stuart A. Kurtz and Janos Simon, The Undecidability of the Generalized Collatz Problem

title: King’s Rule for Definite Integrals
text:
Let \(f\) be integrable on \([a,b]\). King’s rule says
\[
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x)\,dx=\int_a^b f(a+b-x)\,dx.}
\]
It says that reflecting a graph horizontally across the midpoint \(x=(a+b)/2\) does not change its signed area over the interval.
Adding the two equal forms gives the particularly useful identity
\[
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x)\,dx
=\frac12\int_a^b\bigl[f(x)+f(a+b-x)\bigr]\,dx.}
\]

Important: the rule preserves signed area. It therefore remains valid even when parts of the graph lie below the \(x\)-axis.

title: King’s Rule for Definite Integrals
text:
Let \(f\) be integrable on \([a,b]\). King’s rule says
\[
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x)\,dx=\int_a^b f(a+b-x)\,dx.}
\]
It says that reflecting a graph horizontally across the midpoint \(x=(a+b)/2\) does not change its signed area over the interval.
Adding the two equal forms gives the particularly useful identity
\[
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x)\,dx
=\frac12\int_a^b\bigl[f(x)+f(a+b-x)\bigr]\,dx.}
\]

Important: the rule preserves signed area. It therefore remains valid even when parts of the graph lie below the \(x\)-axis.

title: King’s Rule for Definite Integrals
text:
Mathematical proof
Begin with the reflected integral
\[
I=\int_a^b f(a+b-x)\,dx.
\]
Use the substitution
\[
u=a+b-x,\qquad du=-dx.
\]
The limits reverse:
\[
x=a\Longrightarrow u=b,
\qquad
x=b\Longrightarrow u=a.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
I
&=\int_b^a f(u)(-du)\\
&=\int_a^b f(u)\,du\\
&=\int_a^b f(x)\,dx.
\end{aligned}
\]
The final change from \(u\) to \(x\) changes only the name of the dummy variable. This proves the rule.

title: King’s Rule for Definite Integrals
text:
Graphical proof: pair the strips
The transformation
\[
x\longmapsto a+b-x
\]
reflects every point across the midpoint \(m=(a+b)/2\). A narrow strip at \(x=t\) under \(y=f(x)\) has approximate signed area
\[
f(t)\,\Delta x.
\]
After reflection, its partner lies at \(x=a+b-t\). The reflected curve has height
\[
f\bigl(a+b-(a+b-t)\bigr)=f(t),
\]
and reflection preserves the strip width \(\Delta x\). Thus the partner strip has the same signed area. Pairing all such strips from left to right proves that the two integrals are equal.

title: King’s Rule for Definite Integrals
text:
Interactive area proof
Drag directly across the graph, or use the slider. The blue strip is at \(t\) under \(f(x)\); the gold strip is at its reflected position \(a+b-t\) under \(f(a+b-x)\). The shaded regions accumulate equal signed areas.


Reflection position

t = 1.50

 





Original strip: x = 1.50


Reflected strip: x = 4.50


Paired height: —


Accumulated areas: —



Move the handle slowly. At the midpoint the paired strips meet; beyond it they exchange sides.


The applet uses \([a,b]=[0,6]\). Its function is deliberately asymmetric: the equality is caused by reflection, not by choosing a symmetric graph.

title: King’s Rule for Definite Integrals
section: Why the averaged form is powerful
text:
Why the averaged form is powerful
Let
\[
I=\int_a^b f(x)\,dx.
\]
King’s rule gives another expression for the same number:
\[
I=\int_a^b f(a+b-x)\,dx.
\]
Adding and dividing by \(2\) yields
\[
I=\frac12\int_a^b\left[f(x)+f(a+b-x)\right]dx.
\]
This is most useful when the sum inside the brackets simplifies.

title: King’s Rule for Definite Integrals
text:
Example
Evaluate
\[
I=\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{\sin x}{\sin x+\cos x}\,dx.
\]
Here \(a+b=\pi/2\). King’s rule gives
\[
I=\int_0^{\pi/2}
\frac{\cos x}{\cos x+\sin x}\,dx.
\]
Add the two equations:
\[
2I=\int_0^{\pi/2}1\,dx=\frac{\pi}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{I=\frac{\pi}{4}}.
\]

title: King’s Rule for Definite Integrals
text:
Takeaway
King’s rule is simply invariance of a definite integral under reflection of its interval. Algebraically, reversing the limits cancels the minus sign in \(du=-dx\). Geometrically, every signed-area strip has an equal reflected partner.

title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
text:
Let us explore one of the most useful symmetry ideas in definite integration. If \(f\) is integrable on \([a,b]\), King’s property says
\[
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x)\,dx=\int_a^b f(a+b-x)\,dx.}
\]
In simple language: reflecting the graph horizontally across the midpoint \(x=(a+b)/2\) does not change its signed area over the interval.
Adding the two equal forms gives the useful identity
\[
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x)\,dx
=\frac12\int_a^b\bigl[f(x)+f(a+b-x)\bigr],dx.}
\]

Important: the property preserves signed area, so it remains valid even when parts of the graph lie below the \(x\)-axis.

title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
text:
Let us explore one of the most useful symmetry ideas in definite integration. If \(f\) is integrable on \([a,b]\), King’s property says
\[
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x)\,dx=\int_a^b f(a+b-x)\,dx.}
\]
In simple language: reflecting the graph horizontally across the midpoint \(x=(a+b)/2\) does not change its signed area over the interval.
Adding the two equal forms gives the useful identity
\[
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x)\,dx
=\frac12\int_a^b\bigl[f(x)+f(a+b-x)\bigr],dx.}
\]

Important: the property preserves signed area, so it remains valid even when parts of the graph lie below the \(x\)-axis.

title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
text:
Let me prove it mathematically
Begin with the reflected integral
\[
I=\int_a^b f(a+b-x)\,dx.
\]
Now use the substitution
\[
u=a+b-x,\qquad du=-dx.
\]
The limits reverse:
\[
x=a\Longrightarrow u=b,
\qquad
x=b\Longrightarrow u=a.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
I
&=\int_b^a f(u)(-du)\\
&=\int_a^b f(u)\,du\\
&=\int_a^b f(x)\,dx.
\end{aligned}
\]
In the last line, the dummy variable \(u\) has simply been renamed as \(x\). This proves King’s property.

title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
section: Now let us see the graphical proof
text:
Now let us see the graphical proof
Focus on the transformation
\[
x\longmapsto a+b-x.
\]
It reflects every point across the midpoint \(m=(a+b)/2\). Imagine a very narrow strip at \(x=t\) under \(y=f(x)\). Its approximate signed area is
\[
f(t)\,\Delta x.
\]
After reflection, its partner lies at \(x=a+b-t\). The reflected curve has height
\[
f\bigl(a+b-(a+b-t)\bigr)=f(t).
\]
Reflection also preserves the width \(\Delta x\). Therefore, the two strips have exactly the same signed area. Pairing every strip in this way shows that the two complete integrals must be equal.

title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
King’s Property for Definite Integrals
text:
Verifying the picture with Python
The same asymmetric function can be plotted and integrated numerically with Python. This gives an independent computational check of the graphical argument.


Show the Python code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.style.use("dark_background")

a, b = 0, 6
x = np.linspace(a, b, 1000)

def f(x):
    return 1.35 + 0.42 * np.sin(1.55 * x) + 0.16 * x

y_original = f(x)
y_reflected = f(a + b - x)

area_original = np.trapezoid(y_original, x)
area_reflected = np.trapezoid(y_reflected, x)

print(f"Area under f(x):       {area_original:.8f}")
print(f"Area after reflection: {area_reflected:.8f}")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5), facecolor="#0c1521")
ax.set_facecolor("#0c1521")
plt.plot(x, y_original, label=r"$f(x)$", linewidth=2.5)
plt.plot(x, y_reflected, label=r"$f(a+b-x)$", linewidth=2.5)
plt.fill_between(x, y_original, alpha=0.18)
plt.axvline((a + b) / 2, color="gray", linestyle="--", label="midpoint")
plt.xlabel("x")
plt.ylabel("y")
plt.title("A function and its reflection")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(alpha=0.2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


Area under f(x):       11.51982490
Area after reflection: 11.51982490









The two printed values agree up to numerical rounding. Python confirms what the substitution already proved exactly.

title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
section: Why the averaged form is powerful
text:
Why the averaged form is powerful
Let
\[
I=\int_a^b f(x)\,dx.
\]
King’s property gives another expression for the same number:
\[
I=\int_a^b f(a+b-x)\,dx.
\]
Adding and dividing by \(2\) gives
\[
I=\frac12\int_a^b\left[f(x)+f(a+b-x)\right]dx.
\]
This is most useful when the sum inside the brackets simplifies.

title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
section: Let me work through an example
text:
Let me work through an example
Evaluate
\[
I=\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{\sin x}{\sin x+\cos x}\,dx.
\]
Here \(a+b=\pi/2\). King’s property gives
\[
I=\int_0^{\pi/2}
\frac{\cos x}{\cos x+\sin x}\,dx.
\]
Adding the two equations,
\[
2I=\int_0^{\pi/2}1\,dx=\frac{\pi}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{I=\frac{\pi}{4}}.
\]

title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
text:
Takeaway
King’s property tells us that a definite integral is unchanged when its interval is reflected. Algebraically, reversing the limits cancels the minus sign in \(du=-dx\). Geometrically, every signed-area strip has an equal reflected partner. The slider makes these two descriptions visible as the same idea.

title: King’s Property for Definite Integrals
text:
A more powerful JEE-style example
Evaluate
\[
J=\int_0^\pi \frac{x}{1+\sin x}\,dx.
\]
The factor \(x\) makes direct integration look unpleasant. Reflecting \(x\) across \([0,\pi]\) replaces it by \(\pi-x\), while \(\sin(\pi-x)=\sin x\). Thus
\[
J=\int_0^\pi \frac{\pi-x}{1+\sin x}\,dx.
\]
Adding this to the original form gives
\[
2J=\pi\int_0^\pi\frac{dx}{1+\sin x}.
\]
Now rationalise the denominator:
\[
\frac{1}{1+\sin x}
=\frac{1-\sin x}{\cos^2x}
=\sec^2x-\sec x\tan x.
\]
Although the two terms are individually improper at \(x=\pi/2\), their difference has a finite continuous limit there. An antiderivative on each side is \(\tan x-\sec x\), and the combined definite integral equals \(2\). Therefore
\[
2J=2\pi
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\boxed{J=\pi}.
\]
The important move was not the final integration. King’s property first removed the troublesome factor \(x\).
